place: Callicoon CDP, New York, New York

facts.text: Callicoon was renamed Callicoon Depot when the Erie Railroad built its Delaware Division in 1848, making the station the town’s heart.
The first settlers were Dutch hunters from the Hudson Valley in the 1600s, and the name “Kollikoonkill” means Wild Turkey Creek.
A 1888 fire nearly destroyed Main Street, leaving only buildings older than that year, a fact etched into the town’s history.
Callicoon hosts an annual Tractor Parade each June, where over 200 antique and modern tractors march down Main Street.
The last passenger train ran on November 27, 1966, after which tourism grew, bringing campgrounds, canoe liveries, and bed‑and‑breakfast inns.
The Upper Delaware Scenic and Recreational River was established in 1978 to protect the valley’s natural beauty and the Delaware River’s quality.
